items.question_text: What must happen if a departure flight plan has been amended or revised and the airport is using PDC?

choices.text: The amended or revised clearance must be issued verbally to the flight crew rather than transmitted by PDC.
It may still be sent by PDC as long as the route remains continuous.
It may be sent by PDC only if the amended route was generated by automation after the aircraft checked in.
It should be sent by PDC first and then confirmed verbally after pushback.

items.explanation: PDC does not transmit amended or revised flight plans. Those clearances are issued verbally after review for accuracy, completeness, and route continuity.

items.situation: A revised route is received for a departure that was going to be sent by PDC. Another controller wants to transmit the amended flight plan through PDC anyway because the system can display the change, and they do not want to call the flight crew unless the route continuity looks broken.
items.para_context: PDC clearances must be reviewed for accuracy, route integrity, completeness, and continuity. Amended or revised flight plans are not sent by PDC and must be issued verbally to the flight crew.

choices.text: Review the clearance for accuracy and continuity, and verbally issue the revised or amended flight plan instead of transmitting it by PDC.
Transmit the amended route by PDC as long as the route is continuous, because continuity is the only limit on PDC use.
Use PDC for any revision if the automation system generated the display, because automation-generated changes are never considered amended.
Skip the review and send the clearance by PDC first, then correct any route-integrity issue verbally if the pilot questions it.

items.explanation: A revised or amended flight plan is handled verbally, not by PDC, even if the system can display it.

items.question_text: What key capability does CPDLC DCL have that PDC does not?

choices.text: CPDLC DCL may transmit amended or revised flight plans when they can be properly delivered through CPDLC.
CPDLC DCL may transmit any nonoperational information, while PDC is limited to operational content.
CPDLC DCL does not require review for route integrity before transmission.
CPDLC DCL may use free-form text for any field, while PDC is limited to predefined selections.

items.explanation: Unlike PDC, CPDLC DCL may be used to transmit amended or revised flight plans if the revised clearance can be properly delivered through CPDLC.

items.situation: A departure needs a revised clearance and the tower has CPDLC DCL available. One controller assumes any revised plan must be handled by voice because that was true for PDC, while another wants to send a CPDLC DCL that still contains ambiguous route information because the data link can be corrected later if needed.
items.para_context: CPDLC DCL clearances must be reviewed for accuracy, route integrity, completeness, and continuity. CPDLC DCL may transmit amended or revised flight plans, but if the revised clearance cannot be properly delivered through CPDLC it must be issued verbally.

choices.text: Review the clearance for accuracy and route integrity, and use CPDLC only if the revised clearance can be properly delivered that way; otherwise issue it verbally.
Use voice for every amended or revised flight plan because CPDLC DCL never permits those changes.
Send the CPDLC DCL even if the route is not yet complete or understandable, because the flight crew can clarify it later.
Use CPDLC only for advisory text and issue every operational clearance element by voice.

items.explanation: CPDLC DCL can carry a revised clearance, but only if that clearance is complete, understandable, and suitable for delivery by CPDLC.

items.question_text: If no SID is assigned, or the assigned SID does not contain an initial altitude or vertical guidance, what must Selectable Field 5 contain in the DCL application?

choices.text: MAINTAIN (assigned altitude).
CLIMB VIA SID.
NO SID.
SEE SID.

items.explanation: Selectable Field 5 is reserved for the initial altitude assignment when no SID with usable vertical guidance provides that information.

items.situation: A departure has no SID filed or assigned by EAS, no transition is to be assigned, and the clearance will include an initial altitude because there is no vertical guidance from a SID. Another controller wants to leave the SID and Transition fields blank, put the altitude in the CLIMB VIA field, and use the Local Information field for instructions that contradict the rest of the departure clearance.
items.para_context: Each selectable field has a defined purpose. Use NO SID in Field 1 when no SID is assigned, use ---- in Field 2 when no transition is assigned, reserve Field 4 for CLIMB VIA uses tied to a SID, put MAINTAIN (assigned altitude) in Field 5 when needed, and do not let Field 9 contradict the rest of the clearance.

choices.text: Use NO SID in Field 1, ---- in Field 2, place MAINTAIN (assigned altitude) in Field 5, and keep Field 9 from contradicting any other part of the clearance.
Leave the SID and Transition fields blank, use CLIMB VIA in Field 4 even though no SID with vertical guidance is assigned, and let Field 9 restate conflicting local instructions if needed.
Put the initial altitude in Field 3 only, because Field 5 is never used when there is no SID.
Use any field with available space, because the selectable fields are suggestions rather than standardized assignments.

items.explanation: The DCL selectable fields are standardized by purpose and should not be repurposed or used in contradictory ways.

items.question_text: How should a controller handle a CPDLC weather-deviation request if the aircraft still has a climb-via, descend-via, or crossing restriction clearance?

choices.text: Unable the request in CPDLC and revert to voice communications.
Approve the deviation in CPDLC immediately, because pilot-initiated weather requests always take priority over route or altitude constraints.
Leave the request open until the next sector takes the handoff and resolves the restriction conflict.
Use free-text CPDLC to tell the pilot to disregard the crossing restriction and proceed around weather.

items.explanation: When the aircraft still has climb-via, descend-via, or crossing restrictions, the CPDLC weather-deviation request is not handled directly by CPDLC; the controller reverts to voice.

items.situation: A pilot sends a CPDLC weather-deviation request while the aircraft still has a climb-via clearance and a crossing restriction. Another controller wants to approve it by CPDLC immediately, leave an open trajectory-altering clearance in the system through the handoff, and let a noneligible position terminate the CPDLC connection without first coordinating with the sector that has eligibility.
items.para_context: Use voice instead of CPDLC for this weather-deviation request, avoid open trajectory-altering clearances before transfer unless otherwise coordinated, and coordinate with the sector that has eligibility before another position terminates a CPDLC connection.

choices.text: Unable the request in CPDLC and revert to voice, ensure there are no open trajectory-altering clearances before transfer unless otherwise coordinated, and coordinate with the sector that has eligibility before another position terminates the connection.
Approve the deviation by CPDLC because CPDLC should be used for faster handling than voice, and leave the open trajectory change for the next sector to resolve.
Terminate the CPDLC connection from any convenient position without coordination, because eligibility matters only when composing messages.
Use CPDLC for every immediate or expeditious clearance whenever possible so the voice frequency remains clear.

items.explanation: This case combines several limits on CPDLC use: weather-deviation handling with route or altitude restrictions, open TAC before transfer, and eligibility coordination for connection termination.

items.question_text: If a CPDLC clearance being overridden by voice contains multiple elements, what must the controller restate?

choices.text: The entire clearance.
Only the single element that is changing, because the rest of the CPDLC clearance remains active.
Only the altitude or route portion, because speed and holding portions may remain in CPDLC.
Nothing by voice unless the pilot reports the data link message was not received.

items.explanation: When overriding an open CPDLC clearance by voice, a multi-element clearance is restated in full.

items.situation: An aircraft shows an IC mismatch indicator in the FDB and ACL. The aircraft is on your voice frequency. Another controller wants to cancel an open uplink immediately to clear the indication even though the pilot has not yet acknowledged the superseding voice clearance.
items.para_context: The controller on the aircraft's voice frequency verifies the assigned altitude by voice. Abnormal indications are acknowledged only after required coordination, and an open uplink is canceled only after the pilot has received and acknowledged the superseding voice clearance.

choices.text: Use voice to verify the assigned altitude, complete any required coordination before acknowledging the abnormal indication, and do not cancel the open uplink until the pilot has acknowledged the superseding voice clearance.
Acknowledge the abnormal indication immediately and cancel the open uplink first, then verify the altitude later if needed.
Use CPDLC to verify the altitude because the mismatch appeared in the data block, and cancel the uplink as soon as the replacement clearance is typed.
Leave the mismatch unresolved until the next sector takes the handoff, because all sectors can see the abnormal indication anyway.

items.explanation: The mismatch is resolved by voice and coordination first; the uplink is canceled only after the superseding voice clearance has been acknowledged.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: DISREGARD CPDLC ALTITUDE CLEARANCE TO FLIGHT LEVEL THREE FIVE ZERO AND SEND AN UNABLE.
items.display_text: DISREGARD CPDLC ALTITUDE CLEARANCE TO FLIGHT LEVEL THREE FIVE ZERO AND SEND A WILCO
items.explanation: When overriding an open CPDLC clearance by voice, the pilot is told to disregard the CPDLC clearance and send an UNABLE.

items.question_text: What voice action is required when CPDLC service is shut down or fails?

choices.text: Broadcast that CPDLC is no longer in use and tell pilots not to downlink any messages until further advised.
Wait for the next transfer of communications to advise each pilot individually by CPDLC that service has failed.
Advise just the aircraft that already have open uplinks, because the rest of the fleet will see the system failure automatically.
Issue a voice alert only if the shutdown also affects automated altimeter delivery below FL180.

items.explanation: A CPDLC shutdown or failure requires a voice broadcast telling pilots CPDLC is no longer in use and instructing them not to downlink until further advised.

items.situation: A CPDLC shutdown occurs during a busy push. One aircraft also never received an automated altimeter update, and several pilots may still try to send downlinks because they have not heard anything on voice yet.
items.para_context: If the system fails to provide a needed automated altimeter setting, issue the altimeter by voice. During a shutdown or failure, alert pilots by voice, tell them not to downlink until further advised, and resolve open or abnormally closed uplinks by voice.

choices.text: Issue the needed altimeter by voice, broadcast that CPDLC is no longer in use and that pilots should not downlink until further advised, and resolve any open or abnormally closed uplinks by voice.
Wait for the CPDLC system to restore automated altimeter delivery before taking action, because the shutdown message is generated automatically.
Use free-text downlinks to tell each aircraft not to downlink anymore, then clean up the open uplinks after the shutdown ends.
Issue the altimeter only to aircraft already on voice, but leave open or abnormally closed uplinks unresolved because the system will reconcile them at restart.

items.explanation: During a CPDLC shutdown, controllers move the affected work to voice, including shutdown advisories, altimeter issuance when automation fails, and cleanup of open uplinks.

items.question_text: Which speed assignment may be issued by CPDLC in this situation?

choices.text: A speed assignment to an aircraft at FL400 that is not established on a route or procedure with published speed restrictions.
A speed change to an aircraft established on a procedure that already has published speed restrictions, as long as the aircraft is at or above FL390.
A clearance out of holding combined with a speed assignment, because the WILCO response closes both items together.
A speed change issued through advisory free text whenever the pilot can acknowledge it with WILCO.

items.explanation: At or above FL390, a WILCO can satisfy concurrence for a CPDLC speed assignment, but CPDLC is not used for speed changes on procedures that already have published speed restrictions.

items.situation: An aircraft is at FL400 on a procedure with published speed restrictions and later needs further holding instructions. Another controller wants to send the speed change by CPDLC, use advisory free text for a control instruction that requires acknowledgment, and then clear the aircraft out of holding by CPDLC without canceling the climb-via or descend-via clearance first.
items.para_context: Acknowledgment-required control instructions stay out of advisory or free text. CPDLC is not used for speed changes on procedures with published speed restrictions, is not used to clear an aircraft out of holding, and climb-via or descend-via must be canceled before CPDLC holding instructions are issued.

choices.text: Keep acknowledgment-required control instructions out of advisory or free text, do not use CPDLC for the speed change on the published-speed procedure or to clear the aircraft out of holding, and cancel climb-via or descend-via before using CPDLC for holding instructions.
Use CPDLC for the speed change because the aircraft is above FL390, and use free text for the holding clearance so long as the pilot replies WILCO.
Clear the aircraft out of holding by CPDLC first, then cancel climb-via or descend-via later if the aircraft asks for clarification.
Use advisory free text for any message as long as the intent is obvious, because free text and standard CPDLC messages are operationally interchangeable.

items.explanation: This paragraph limits several CPDLC uses at once: acknowledgment-required control instructions, speed changes on published-speed procedures, clearing out of holding, and holding instructions during climb-via or descend-via.

items.question_text: When CPDLC is available and a connected aircraft is operating outside VHF coverage, what is the primary means of communication?

choices.text: CPDLC, with a voice backup available.
Voice, with CPDLC used only if the frequency becomes congested.
Free-text CPDLC only, because standard message sets are reserved for domestic operations.
Either medium at the controller's discretion, because no primary method is specified outside VHF coverage.

items.explanation: Outside VHF coverage, available CPDLC becomes the primary means of communication, but a voice backup still has to exist.

items.situation: A CPDLC-connected aircraft is outside VHF coverage. Another controller wants to keep using voice as the primary medium because it is familiar, reply by voice to the pilot's CPDLC downlink, and send a free-text message even though a standard CPDLC message element is available.
items.para_context: When CPDLC is available outside VHF coverage, it is primary. A voice backup must still exist. When a pilot communicates via CPDLC, the reply should be via CPDLC, and the standard message set should be used instead of free text when possible.

choices.text: Use CPDLC as the primary communication method, ensure a voice backup exists, reply via CPDLC, and use the standard message set instead of free text when available.
Use voice as the primary method whenever possible outside VHF coverage, because CPDLC is only a backup for oceanic aircraft.
Reply by voice to any CPDLC downlink so the pilot hears an immediate answer, and use free text whenever it is shorter than the standard message set.
Use CPDLC as the primary method only if there is no voice backup available, because the backup requirement applies only when voice is primary.

items.explanation: Oceanic CPDLC still uses a voice backup, but CPDLC is primary outside VHF coverage and the message exchange should stay in the CPDLC medium when possible.

items.question_text: How must transfer of communications be handled when the receiving facility is not CPDLC capable?

choices.text: In accordance with local directives and Letters of Agreement.
By automatic data link transfer within facility-adapted parameters.
By pilot choice between voice and data link after handoff is accepted.
By CPDLC free text unless the receiving facility rejects the transfer.

items.explanation: Automatic transfer applies when the receiving facility is CPDLC capable. If it is not, local directives and LOAs govern the transfer.

items.situation: You are transferring communications to the next facility. One receiving sector is CPDLC capable, while another possible receiving facility is not CPDLC capable. A controller wants to handle both transfers the same way without checking facility capability or the local LOA.
items.para_context: When the receiving facility is CPDLC capable, data link transfer is automatic within adapted parameters. When it is not CPDLC capable, transfer of communications must follow local directives and the applicable LOA.

choices.text: Let the transfer occur automatically when the receiving facility is CPDLC capable, but if the receiving facility is not CPDLC capable, make the transfer in accordance with local directives and the applicable LOA.
Handle every transfer manually by voice, because automatic data link transfer is only optional even when both facilities are CPDLC capable.
Assume automatic transfer works for every receiving facility and use local directives only if the pilot reports a failure after transfer.
Treat local directives and LOAs as advisory only, because CPDLC capability alone governs the transfer method.

items.explanation: The controller has to distinguish between CPDLC-capable and non-capable receiving facilities instead of treating all transfers the same way.

items.clearance: UNITED 234, CPDLC FAILURE, MAINTAIN FLIGHT LEVEL THREE THREE ZERO.

choices.text: United 234, CPDLC failure, maintain flight level three three zero.
United 234, voice failure, maintain flight level three three zero.
CPDLC failure, maintain flight level three three zero.
United 234, CPDLC failure, maintain flight level three zero zero.

items.explanation: Initial voice contact after a CPDLC failure uses the prefatory phrase '(Call Sign) CPDLC failure, (message).'

items.situation: An automated data link transfer fails. You cannot complete the transfer manually right away, and there is also uncertainty whether a previously issued CPDLC clearance was actually delivered to the aircraft. Another controller wants to stop protecting the airspace tied to that clearance and wait for the pilot to call if there is a problem.
items.para_context: If automated transfer fails, attempt it manually. If that still cannot be completed, advise the pilot to log on to the next facility and send an End Service message. If there is uncertainty that a CPDLC clearance was delivered, continue protecting the associated airspace until an appropriate operational response is received and take action to confirm the clearance status.

choices.text: Attempt the transfer manually, advise the pilot to log on to the next facility and send an End Service message if the transfer still cannot be completed, and continue protecting the airspace associated with the uncertain clearance until an appropriate operational response is received.
Terminate CPDLC immediately, stop protecting the airspace once the transfer automation fails, and wait for the pilot to report whether the clearance was received.
Assume the transfer and clearance both succeeded because the aircraft remains CPDLC capable, so no further action is required unless the next facility rejects the handoff.
Use only voice for future instructions, but do not advise the pilot to log on again or send End Service because the failed transfer will eventually time out on its own.

items.explanation: A failed automated transfer triggers a manual attempt and possibly pilot logon/EOS instructions, and uncertainty about clearance delivery means the airspace tied to that clearance remains protected until operational closure is received.
